scenario: REST Connector needs to send custom headers (e.g., X-Tenant-Id, X-Request-Id). Team wonders how to configure.
question: REST Connector custom headers?

options.text: md:144-152): Property name in Element Template UI: Headers (Optional, under HTTP request configuration). a. io/" }. Reserved process-variable names to avoid collisions (:9-13): body, url, method, headers, authentication, queryParameters, connectionTimeoutInSeconds, readTimeoutInSeconds -- do NOT create a process variable named headers. , "X-Tenant-Id": tenantId). Content-Type default (:154-158): if not set, connector auto-sets application/json. For multipart upload, set Content-Type: multipart/form-data. NAME}} (per Set 14 Q45 / Pattern #11 syntax). headers. (per :203).

The REST Connector in Camunda 8 only supports an Authorization header and no other custom HTTP headers -- this approach has limitations. The REST Connector's Headers property accepts any HTTP headers as a FEEL context (map): = { "Content-Type": "application/json", "X-Tenant-Id": tenantId, "X-Request-Id": requestId }. Any valid HTTP header name can be used as a context key. The Authorization header for Bearer tokens is a separate authentication configuration in the Authentication section -- it does not restrict what custom headers can be added in the Headers property.

HTTP headers in the REST Connector are fixed at design time and cannot use process variables or dynamic FEEL expressions -- this approach has limitations. The Headers property value is a FEEL context where each value can reference process variables: = { "X-Tenant-Id": tenantId, "X-Correlation-Id": correlationId }. Dynamic header values are evaluated at execution time against current process variable values. NAME}} syntax. This enables completely dynamic header composition driven by process state, tenant configuration, or runtime context without any hardcoded values in the process model.

Setting custom HTTP headers on outbound REST calls requires building a custom Connector -- this overstates the complexity. The built-in REST Connector's Headers property already supports arbitrary custom HTTP headers via a FEEL context expression. , a team-specific GraphQL wrapper with automatic error unwrapping), not for simply adding headers. The REST Connector with proper FEEL context in the Headers property handles the vast majority of real-world header customization needs without any additional development.

a.text: Correct. Custom REST Connector headers via FEEL Map: Configuration -- in the Headers field of the REST Connector property panel, enter a FEEL context expression (rest.md:146 verbatim -- "the HTTP headers can be specified using the [FEEL Map] data type"): `text = { "X-Tenant-Id": tenantId, "X-Request-Id": uuid(), "X-Custom": "value" } Behind the scenes, the Element Template binding stores this as a connector input (NOT a raw zeebe:taskHeader -- REST Connector reads it from input context). Reserved word collision: do NOT create a process variable named headers (:11). Common headers: Authentication: Authorization: Bearer token, Basic auth. X-API-Key: custom API key. Content negotiation: Content-Type: request body format. Accept: expected response format. Tracing: X-Request-Id: correlation ID for distributed tracing. traceparent: W3C Trace Context. Tenant routing: X-Tenant-Id: multi-tenant routing. Custom business: X-Customer-Id, X-Order-Id, etc. Dynamic values via FEEL: feel headers: { "X-Tenant-Id": tenantId, "X-Request-Id": uuid(), "X-User-Id": user.id, "X-Region": customer.region } For tracing: feel headers: { "traceparent": "00-" + traceId + "-" + spanId + "-01" } Propagate trace context through downstream calls. For static + dynamic mix: feel headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json", "X-API-Version": "v2", "X-Customer-Id": customer.id } Security headers: Authorization: Bearer / Basic. Secrets binding: ={{secrets.API_TOKEN}} (per Set 14 Q45). Don't inline credentials. For multi-tenant: X-Tenant-Id common pattern. Header per request: identifies tenant. Service uses for routing: which DB / config. For idempotency: Idempotency-Key: unique per logical request. Helps with retries (per Set 13 Q10). Generate: uuid() or business-meaningful ID. Best practices: Use standard headers where applicable: Authorization, Content-Type, etc. Custom headers prefixed X-: convention (though no longer strictly required by RFC). Document headers: in API contract / Element Template. Sensitive headers via secrets: never inline. Element Templates with header config -- the OOTB REST Connector template already exposes a Headers text-area (FEEL context input); custom Element Templates that wrap the REST Connector should mirror the same pattern (single property, FEEL context value) rather than per-header zeebe:taskHeader bindings: json { "label": "Headers", "description": "Map of HTTP request headers", "type": "String", "feel": "required", "binding": { "type": "zeebe:input", "name": "headers" }, "value": "= { \"X-Tenant-Id\": tenantId, \"X-Request-Id\": uuid() }" } Note: binding type is zeebe:input with name: "headers" (connector input), NOT zeebe:taskHeader. The connector reads it from the input context. Response headers: Connector response may include headers. Access via result expression: response.headers.x-rate-limit-remaining`. For tracking rate limits, etc. Anti-patterns: Hardcoding secrets in headers: use secrets binding. Long-lived static API keys without rotation: security risk. No tracing headers: harder to debug distributed flows. For the scenario (X-Tenant-Id, X-Request-Id): Configure headers map. Use FEEL for dynamic values (tenantId from variable, uuid() for request ID). Document for partner.
